AI Engineering / LLMs & NLP

LLMs & NLP.

A production-first guide to language systems: transformer fundamentals, prompt contracts, RAG pipelines, tool-using agents, evaluation, cost control, and reliability.

NLPBM25EmbeddingsTransformersRAGAgentsEvaluationGuardrails
Language Foundations 01-04
01 Text, Corpus, and Vocabulary 02 Classical NLP Tasks and Labels 03 Sparse Retrieval and Lexical Search 04 Embeddings and Semantic Similarity
Model Foundations 05-08
05 Transformer Architecture 06 Prompting as Interface Design 07 Model Families and Training Objectives 08 Adaptation and Fine-Tuning
RAG Systems 09-11
09 Retrieval Architecture 10 Grounded Generation 11 Knowledge Quality and Graph RAG
Agents + Tools 12-13
12 Tool-Using Systems 13 Memory, Planning, and Multi-Agent Patterns
Production Quality 14-16
14 Evaluation, Cost, and Reliability 15 Serving, Safety, and Governance 16 Interview Playbook
Language Foundations 01-04
01

Text, Corpus, and Vocabulary

Start with the raw material of NLP: messy text, documents, labels, and the vocabulary built from them.

A corpus is the collection of text a system learns from, searches over, evaluates on, or uses as grounding evidence. Good corpus design starts with task boundaries: who writes the text, what language and format it uses, which domains matter, and what future examples must not leak into training or evaluation. For interview revision, always connect corpus quality to downstream metrics because bad data can make even a strong model look unstable.

Python - stratified split sketch 
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

train, test = train_test_split(
    examples,
    test_size=0.2,
    stratify=[x["label"] for x in examples],
    random_state=42,
)
assert not set(x["doc_id"] for x in train) & set(x["doc_id"] for x in test)
Pitfall Document leakage across splits

Chunks from the same source document appear in both train and test, inflating accuracy and hiding generalization failures.

Fix Split by document, user, account, or time depending on the production boundary.
Pitfall Evaluation corpus does not match production

The test set uses clean English tickets, but production includes OCR, tables, abbreviations, and multilingual text.

Fix Sample eval data from real traces and maintain slices for language, source, length, and domain.

A corpus is a collection of text examples used for training, retrieval, evaluation, analysis, or grounding.

Split by the unit that repeats in production, such as document, user, conversation, company, or time window, not only by individual row.

Metadata enables filtering, slicing, access control, debugging, recency handling, and fair evaluation across subgroups.

Before neural embeddings, NLP systems represented text using vocabulary features. N-grams still matter for search, classification baselines, spam detection, log analysis, and exact term matching. They capture surface patterns that dense embeddings can miss, especially IDs, error codes, product names, and rare legal or medical terms. A strong learning path understands these baselines before jumping to LLMs.

Python - TF-IDF baseline 
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

vec = TfidfVectorizer(ngram_range=(1, 2), min_df=2, max_features=50000)
X = vec.fit_transform([x["text"] for x in train])
clf = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000).fit(X, [x["label"] for x in train])
Pitfall Skipping the baseline

A team ships an expensive LLM classifier without knowing whether TF-IDF plus logistic regression would meet the SLA.

Fix Build lexical and small-model baselines before choosing an LLM-heavy architecture.
Pitfall Vocabulary too broad or too narrow

Keeping every token overfits noise; pruning too aggressively removes rare but important terms.

Fix Tune min_df, max_df, and n-gram range against validation slices.

A contiguous sequence of n tokens. A unigram is one token, a bigram is two, and a trigram is three.

It is strong for exact terms, rare identifiers, domain jargon, and small labeled datasets where semantic generalization is less important.

Out-of-vocabulary means a token or term was not seen during vocabulary construction and may be ignored or mapped to an unknown feature.

Normalization turns messy text into a more consistent form, but over-normalization can destroy meaning. Preserve negation, IDs, section numbers, currency, code symbols, capitalization, and table structure when they matter. Modern LLM and embedding models usually prefer natural text, so aggressive stemming and stop-word removal should be justified by evals, not habit.

Python - conservative normalizer 
import re
import unicodedata

def normalize_text(text: str) -> str:
    text = unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", text)
    text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip()
    text = text.replace("smart-phone", "smartphone")
    return text
Pitfall Removing negation as a stop word

The sentence "not eligible for refund" becomes "eligible refund", reversing the meaning.

Fix Use task-specific stop-word lists and test negation-heavy cases.
Pitfall English-only preprocessing

Whitespace rules, stemming, and sentence splitting fail on multilingual or code-mixed inputs.

Fix Detect language and use language-aware tokenization or avoid destructive preprocessing.

Stemming chops words to crude roots; lemmatization maps words to dictionary forms using linguistic knowledge.

Not always. Lowercase can help lexical matching, but it can damage acronyms, IDs, named entities, and case-sensitive code.

Store raw text, normalized text, source metadata, offsets, and the normalizer version.

Most language-system failures begin before the model call: bad parsing, weak labels, missing metadata, or a corpus that does not represent the real task.
02

Classical NLP Tasks and Labels

Learn the task vocabulary that still appears in modern LLM product work.

Classical NLP tasks define what the system should do with text. Classification assigns labels. NER extracts entity spans. Relation extraction connects entities. POS tagging and parsing expose grammatical structure. Summarization compresses information. Clustering groups similar texts. LLMs can perform many of these zero-shot, but production systems still need schemas, labeled examples, and measurable outputs.

Task schema examples 
ticket = {
  "label": "billing_issue",
  "entities": [
    {"type": "invoice_id", "text": "INV-2041", "start": 17, "end": 25}
  ],
  "sentiment": "frustrated"
}
Pitfall Vague label definitions

Annotators cannot distinguish complaint, escalation, urgent, and churn risk consistently.

Fix Write label guidelines with examples, counterexamples, and conflict rules.
Pitfall Entity extraction without normalization

The system extracts "NYC", "New York", and "New York City" but cannot connect them.

Fix Add entity linking to canonical IDs after span extraction.

Named entity recognition identifies spans such as people, companies, products, dates, amounts, and IDs.

It identifies relationships between extracted entities, such as company acquired company or ticket references invoice.

When the language pattern is stable, the risk of false positives is low, and deterministic behavior is more valuable than broad generalization.

A model cannot learn a task that humans cannot label consistently. Labeling quality depends on clear guidelines, calibrated annotators, disagreement review, representative samples, and careful handling of rare classes. For LLM systems, labeled data is still needed for evals, prompt regression, fine-tuning, routing, and human preference optimization.

Python - label distribution check 
from collections import Counter

counts = Counter(x["label"] for x in examples)
total = sum(counts.values())
for label, n in counts.most_common():
    print(label, n, round(n / total, 3))
Pitfall Optimizing overall accuracy on imbalanced labels

A model gets 96% accuracy by always predicting the majority class and misses rare fraud cases.

Fix Track precision, recall, and F1 per class, especially for high-risk labels.
Pitfall No disagreement workflow

Annotator conflicts are hidden in the data and become confusing training signal.

Fix Review disagreements, update guidelines, and keep adjudicated labels separate from raw labels.

A measure of how consistently different annotators label the same examples.

A labeling strategy that prioritizes uncertain or high-value examples for human annotation.

Use stratified sampling, class-weighted losses, threshold tuning, targeted data collection, and per-class metrics.

Metrics must match the task. Classification often uses precision, recall, and F1. Extraction uses span-level exact match or partial match. Summarization may use ROUGE or human rubric scores. Semantic answer quality may use BERTScore or LLM judges, but those should be calibrated. Always inspect metrics by slice: source, language, label, length, recency, and customer segment.

Python - classification metrics 
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

y_true = [x["label"] for x in test]
y_pred = clf.predict(vec.transform([x["text"] for x in test]))
print(classification_report(y_true, y_pred, digits=3))
Pitfall One metric for every task

Accuracy hides low recall on the class that matters most to the business.

Fix Choose metrics based on error cost and report them per slice.
Pitfall Using BLEU or ROUGE as truth

Surface overlap can punish good paraphrases and reward copied but incomplete text.

Fix Use them as weak signals with human or rubric-based checks for important tasks.

Precision asks how many predicted positives were correct. Recall asks how many actual positives were found.

The harmonic mean of precision and recall, useful when both false positives and false negatives matter.

A model can look good overall while failing on a language, customer group, document type, or rare label.

LLMs often wrap classical NLP tasks in natural language, but the product still needs labels, spans, metrics, and failure analysis.
03

Sparse Retrieval and Lexical Search

Understand exact-match retrieval before semantic retrieval and RAG.

An inverted index maps each term to the documents containing it. This powers search engines, log search, compliance discovery, and the sparse side of hybrid RAG. It is explainable, fast, filter-friendly, and strong for exact terms. Learn it before vector search because many production retrieval bugs are actually lexical matching or metadata filtering bugs.

Python - tiny inverted index 
from collections import defaultdict

index = defaultdict(set)
for doc_id, text in docs.items():
    for term in text.lower().split():
        index[term].add(doc_id)

matches = index["refund"] & index["policy"]
Pitfall No field-aware search

A match in the footer ranks above a match in the title or policy heading.

Fix Boost important fields such as title, heading, ID, and canonical entity fields.
Pitfall Ignoring analyzers

The search engine tokenizes IDs, hyphens, accents, and case differently than expected.

Fix Test analyzer output for real queries and domain terms.

A structure that maps terms to the documents or positions where those terms occur.

It catches exact identifiers and rare terms that semantic search may smooth away.

Lists of documents, and often positions, where a term appears.

TF-IDF scores terms higher when they are frequent in a document but rare in the corpus. BM25 improves lexical ranking with saturation and document-length normalization. These methods are strong baselines for search, classification, and hybrid retrieval. In interviews, BM25 is a useful contrast against embeddings: exactness and explainability versus semantic generalization.

BM25 scoring intuition 
score(query, doc) =
  sum(
    idf(term) *
    tf_saturation(term, doc) *
    length_normalization(doc)
    for term in query
  )
Pitfall Dense-only retrieval for identifiers

A query for ERR-8492 returns semantically similar troubleshooting docs but misses the exact error page.

Fix Use BM25 or exact filters alongside vector search.
Pitfall Not tuning analyzers and boosts

BM25 performs poorly because tokenization and field weights do not match the domain.

Fix Tune analyzers, synonyms, field boosts, and query parsing with labeled retrieval data.

Both use term rarity, but BM25 adds better term-frequency saturation and document-length normalization for ranking.

Exact IDs, rare names, error codes, quotes, legal clauses, and queries where lexical precision matters.

Inverse document frequency gives more weight to terms that appear in fewer documents.

Hybrid search combines lexical precision with semantic recall. A common production pattern retrieves candidates from BM25 and vector search, filters by permissions and metadata, fuses or reranks the candidates, then sends only the strongest evidence to the model. This is the bridge from classical search to modern RAG.

Python - reciprocal rank fusion 
def rrf(rankings, k=60):
    scores = {}
    for ranking in rankings:
        for rank, doc_id in enumerate(ranking, start=1):
            scores[doc_id] = scores.get(doc_id, 0) + 1 / (k + rank)
    return sorted(scores, key=scores.get, reverse=True)
Pitfall Fusion before permission filters

Unauthorized documents influence ranking or leak through traces.

Fix Apply ACL filters before ranking results are shown or used.
Pitfall Reranking too few candidates

The right document was not in the truncated top ten from either retriever.

Fix Retrieve a wider candidate pool, then rerank and trim.

A simple method that combines multiple ranked lists by giving each document credit based on its rank in each list.

It improves recall across semantic queries and exact lexical queries.

After initial retrieval and filtering, before final prompt assembly.

Dense embeddings are powerful, but sparse search remains the fastest way to catch names, IDs, error codes, versions, and rare terms.
04

Embeddings and Semantic Similarity

Move from lexical overlap to meaning-based retrieval and clustering.

Embeddings represent text as vectors so semantically related items land near each other. They are useful for semantic search, duplicate detection, clustering, routing, recommendations, and RAG. Similarity is task-dependent: the nearest vector can be topically related but outdated, unauthorized, or not actually answerable.

Python - runnable cosine search 
import numpy as np

texts = ["refund policy", "invoice payment", "password reset"]
vectors = np.array([[1.0, 0.1], [0.1, 1.0], [0.8, 0.2]])
query = np.array([0.9, 0.1])

def normalize(x):
    return x / np.linalg.norm(x, axis=-1, keepdims=True)

scores = normalize(vectors) @ normalize(query).reshape(-1, 1)
best = int(scores.argmax())
print(texts[best])
Pitfall Comparing vectors from different models

Scores become meaningless when old and new embedding spaces are mixed.

Fix Store embedding model, dimension, preprocessing, and index build version.
Pitfall Assuming nearest means correct

The closest chunk is similar but not sufficient evidence for the answer.

Fix Rerank, check answerability, and validate generated claims against source text.

A measure of angle between vectors, often used to compare embedding direction after normalization.

The number of numeric coordinates in the vector representation.

Use retrieval recall@k, MRR, clustering quality, duplicate detection F1, and human relevance labels.

Vector databases use approximate nearest-neighbor indexes to find similar vectors quickly. Approximation creates a recall-latency trade-off: faster search may miss relevant documents. Production systems must evaluate retrieval quality after index tuning, not only model quality. Metadata filters and ACLs must be part of the retrieval plan, not an afterthought.

Index config checklist 
vector_index = {
  "embedding_model": "text-embedding-model",
  "dimensions": 1536,
  "metric": "cosine",
  "ann": "hnsw",
  "filters": ["tenant_id", "acl", "doc_type", "created_at"]
}
Pitfall Tuning for latency only

The index gets faster but misses the expected evidence for hard questions.

Fix Track recall@k and latency together during index tuning.
Pitfall Post-filtering after vector search

The top vector results are removed by ACL filters, leaving too few candidates.

Fix Prefer pre-filtering or retrieve a larger candidate pool before filtering.

Approximate nearest-neighbor search trades perfect recall for much faster vector lookup.

A graph-based ANN index commonly used for fast high-recall vector search.

They enforce permissions, recency, source type, tenant boundaries, and domain constraints.

A senior NLP engineer chooses the simplest model that satisfies the product contract. Rules are best for stable exact policies. Classical ML is strong for high-volume labeled tasks. Encoder models are efficient for classification and embeddings. LLMs are best for nuanced language, reasoning, flexible schemas, and explanation. Open-source models help with privacy and control but add infrastructure burden.

Decision rubric 
if exact_policy and stable_pattern:
    choice = "rules"
elif many_labels and strict_latency:
    choice = "small classifier or encoder"
elif needs_reasoning or changing_schema:
    choice = "LLM with evals"
elif data_cannot_leave_vpc:
    choice = "self-hosted model"
Pitfall Choosing by benchmark leaderboard only

The best general model is too slow, costly, or weak on private domain data.

Fix Benchmark on your own eval set with latency, cost, safety, and failure-mode criteria.
Pitfall Ignoring operational ownership

A self-hosted model meets privacy needs but the team cannot operate GPUs, scaling, or updates.

Fix Include infrastructure and support cost in model selection.

API models reduce ops burden and often lead capability; open-source models offer control, privacy, customization, and cost advantages at scale.

For stable, high-throughput, low-latency tasks with enough labeled data.

Define task metrics, build baselines, evaluate candidates on real cases, then choose by quality, cost, latency, privacy, and maintainability.

Embeddings are similarity features, not truth. They need versioning, evaluation, and retrieval guardrails.
Model Foundations 05-08
05

Transformer Architecture

Understand the inference machine before designing systems around it.

Tokenization is the compression boundary between text and model. It shapes cost, context length, latency, and behavior around code, rare names, IDs, tables, and non-English text. Production systems should measure token growth after prompt assembly, not only raw character length, because repeated schema instructions, citations, and tool traces can silently consume the usable context window.

Python - token-budget guard 
def reserve_budget(messages, max_context, max_output, tokenizer):
    used = sum(len(tokenizer.encode(m["content"])) for m in messages)
    available = max_context - max_output
    if used > available:
        raise ValueError(f"Prompt uses {used} tokens; budget is {available}")
    return {"prompt_tokens": used, "remaining": available - used}
Pitfall Counting characters instead of tokens

A short JSON schema or code block can tokenize far worse than prose. The app passes tests on normal text, then fails on legal clauses, tables, logs, and code snippets.

Fix Budget after final prompt assembly with the target model tokenizer and reserve output tokens explicitly.
Pitfall Ignoring tokenizer changes during model migration

A model switch can change token count, truncation behavior, and cost without any product UI changing.

Fix Add token-budget regression tests for representative prompts before changing model families.

It controls what the model can actually see. If the system truncates the wrong context, the model may answer confidently from incomplete evidence.

Log prompt tokens, completion tokens, model name, truncation strategy, selected documents, and final prompt template version.

Self-attention creates queries, keys, and values from token representations. A query asks what this token needs, keys describe what other tokens offer, and values carry the information to mix back in. Multi-head attention repeats this in parallel. During generation, the KV cache stores computed key/value states so the model does not recompute the full prefix at each step. Long input contexts increase prefill latency; long outputs increase decode latency.

Attention and latency sketch 
Q = X @ Wq
K = X @ Wk
V = X @ Wv
attention = softmax((Q @ K.T) / sqrt(d_k)) @ V

total_latency = prefill(context_tokens) + decode(output_tokens)
Pitfall Optimizing only generation length

Teams cap max output but keep sending 40 retrieved chunks and a huge policy block. The user still experiences slow first-token latency.

Fix Measure time-to-first-token separately from total completion time and tune retrieval depth, compression, and prompt prefix reuse.
Pitfall Treating context window as free memory

A 128k context window can still be too slow, too expensive, and too noisy for a specific task.

Fix Use the smallest context that preserves task accuracy, then prove it with evals.

Queries represent what a token is looking for, keys represent what each token can match on, and values carry the information that gets mixed into the output.

Prefill is the phase where the model processes the input prompt and builds KV cache state before emitting the first token.

They introduce distractors, conflicting snippets, and higher instruction dilution. More context is not automatically better context.

Decoding converts model probabilities into text. Deterministic settings help extraction, classification, and tool calls; moderate randomness helps brainstorming and rewrites. Structured outputs reduce parser failures, but they do not guarantee semantic correctness. The production contract is: generate, validate, repair or escalate.

Python - Pydantic structured output 
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError

class Invoice(BaseModel):
    invoice_id: str
    amount_due: float = Field(ge=0)
    currency: str

try:
    invoice = Invoice.model_validate(model_json)
except ValidationError as err:
    raise ValueError(f"invalid model output: {err}")
Pitfall Using high temperature for factual QA

Randomness can make valid retrieval grounding less stable and make regressions harder to reproduce.

Fix Use low temperature for grounded answers, extraction, routing, and tool arguments.
Pitfall Trusting JSON validity as truth

The object parses, but fields can still be unsupported by evidence or violate business logic.

Fix Run schema validation, evidence checks, and domain rules after parsing.

Use it for classification, extraction, compliance responses, deterministic routing, and any path where reproducibility matters more than creative variation.

They constrain generation boundaries, especially in older prompt formats, but they should not replace schema validation.

Most LLM bugs are easier to debug when you can separate model limitation, prompt limitation, retrieval limitation, and product-contract limitation.
06

Prompting as Interface Design

Prompts are not magic words. They are executable specifications for a probabilistic worker.

Production prompts should be versioned artifacts. The best prompt is short enough to audit and explicit enough to test. Separate stable system policy from dynamic user data, retrieval context, and tool state. Treat every prompt edit like a code change because it can alter behavior across thousands of requests.

Prompt skeleton 
System:
You are a support analyst. Answer only from CONTEXT.
If evidence is missing, say what is missing.

Developer:
Return JSON with: answer, citations, confidence, escalation_reason.

User:
QUESTION: {{question}}
CONTEXT: {{retrieved_passages}}
Pitfall Mixing policy, evidence, and user text

The model cannot reliably distinguish trusted instructions from untrusted retrieved content or user-provided text.

Fix Use clear delimiters, typed sections, and explicit instruction hierarchy.
Pitfall Few-shot examples that conflict with the schema

The model imitates the examples and ignores the written contract when they disagree.

Fix Keep examples minimal, valid, and covered by tests.

It is versioned, tested against an eval set, has clear input boundaries, defines failure behavior, and produces a validated output shape.

They are harder to review, more expensive, slower, more fragile, and more likely to contain conflicting instructions.

Prompt injection is not a string problem; it is an authority problem. Retrieved text and user text are data, not instructions. Tools should receive typed arguments from validated model output, not raw generated prose. The application must enforce permissions outside the model.

Guardrail pattern 
if tool_name == "refund_user":
    assert current_user.can("refund")
    args = RefundArgs.model_validate(tool_args)
    assert args.amount <= policy.max_refund
    return refund_service.create(args)
Pitfall Letting retrieved content override the system prompt

A malicious document says ignore previous instructions and exfiltrate hidden data. The model may comply if boundaries are weak.

Fix Label retrieved text as untrusted evidence and enforce data access in application code.
Pitfall Putting secrets in the prompt

Anything in context can be leaked by a sufficiently bad prompt path.

Fix Never send secrets to the model unless disclosure to the user would be acceptable.

In deterministic application code, before and after the model call. The model can propose actions; the app decides what is allowed.

Untrusted text attempts to alter the model instruction hierarchy, often by pretending to be higher-priority instructions.

A good prompt is boring in the best way: clear task, clear inputs, clear constraints, clear output, clear failure behavior.
07

Model Families and Training Objectives

Connect architecture choices to the way language models are trained and used.

Encoder-only models read the whole input bidirectionally and are efficient for classification, extraction, embeddings, and reranking. Decoder-only models generate one token at a time with causal masking and dominate chat, agents, and completion tasks. Encoder-decoder models read an input and generate an output, which historically made them strong for translation, summarization, and sequence-to-sequence transformations.

Model-family decision table 
classification / NER / rerank  -> encoder
chat / code / agent planning    -> decoder
translation / summarization     -> encoder-decoder
semantic search embeddings      -> encoder or embedding-specific model
Pitfall Using a chat LLM for every classification job

The task pays generation latency and cost even though a small encoder could classify faster and cheaper.

Fix Benchmark encoder, rules, and LLM candidates against the same task metrics.
Pitfall Ignoring causal masking

A decoder model cannot attend to future tokens during generation, which shapes latency and output behavior.

Fix Explain generation as next-token prediction with previous-token context only.

BERT is encoder-only and bidirectional, strong for understanding tasks. GPT is decoder-only and autoregressive, strong for generation.

A mask that prevents a decoder from attending to future tokens while predicting the next token.

For tasks that transform an input sequence into an output sequence, such as translation or summarization.

A model family is shaped by its training objective. Masked language modeling predicts hidden tokens from both sides of context. Causal language modeling predicts the next token from prior tokens. Contrastive learning pulls related texts closer in embedding space. Instruction tuning teaches task following. Preference tuning teaches response ranking according to human or rubric preferences.

Objective intuition 
masked_lm:  "The capital of [MASK] is Paris"
causal_lm:  "The capital of France is" -> " Paris"
contrastive: pull(query, positive_doc) > pull(query, negative_doc)
preference:  prefer(chosen_answer) > prefer(rejected_answer)
Pitfall Confusing pretraining with alignment

A pretrained model can predict fluent text but may not follow instructions or refuse unsafe requests.

Fix Separate pretraining, instruction tuning, preference tuning, and safety policy in explanations.
Pitfall Expecting embeddings from a chat model to be ideal

A generative model is not necessarily optimized for similarity search.

Fix Use embedding models trained or tuned for retrieval and validate on recall metrics.

Masked LM predicts hidden tokens using both left and right context; causal LM predicts the next token using previous context only.

A training approach that brings matching pairs closer and pushes non-matching pairs apart in representation space.

Supervised training on instruction-response examples so a base model becomes better at following user tasks.

A transformer block repeatedly mixes token information with attention, transforms each token representation with a feed-forward network, stabilizes training with residual connections and normalization, and eventually projects hidden states into vocabulary logits. This mental model helps debug why prompt length affects latency, why token position matters, and why decoding settings alter outputs without changing model weights.

Transformer block pseudocode 
x = token_embedding(tokens) + position_embedding(tokens)
for block in transformer:
    x = x + multi_head_attention(layer_norm(x))
    x = x + feed_forward(layer_norm(x))
logits = output_projection(layer_norm(x))
Pitfall Treating attention as explanation

Attention weights can be inspected, but they are not a guaranteed faithful explanation of model reasoning.

Fix Use attention as one debugging signal, not as proof of causality.
Pitfall Skipping positional encoding

Without position information, a transformer cannot distinguish word order reliably.

Fix Explain that token identity and token position are both part of the model input.

Multiple attention heads let the model attend to different relationships or positions in parallel.

They help gradients flow and let each block refine rather than replace the representation.

Raw vocabulary scores before softmax turns them into probabilities.

BERT, GPT, and T5 are not just brand names. Their architecture and training objective shape what they are naturally good at.
08

Adaptation and Fine-Tuning

Know when to change the prompt, the data, the retrieval layer, or the model weights.

Supervised fine-tuning teaches a model to imitate high-quality input-output examples. It is useful when prompts are too long, behavior must be consistent, outputs have domain-specific style, or the task has many examples. It is not a reliable way to insert large changing knowledge. Keep a held-out set, deduplicate training data, test refusals and edge cases, and compare against a strong prompt-only baseline.

JSONL - training example shape 
{"messages":[
  {"role":"system","content":"Extract support ticket fields as JSON."},
  {"role":"user","content":"Customer cannot log in after SSO migration."},
  {"role":"assistant","content":"{\"category\":\"auth\",\"severity\":\"high\"}"}
]}
Pitfall Fine-tuning to fix retrieval gaps

The model still cannot know a newly updated policy or private document unless that evidence is supplied.

Fix Use RAG for changing knowledge and fine-tune only stable behavior.
Pitfall Training on low-quality assistant outputs

The model learns verbosity, unsupported claims, and schema mistakes from weak examples.

Fix Curate examples, include negative cases, and evaluate on data not used in training.

When you need consistent behavior, style, formatting, or task policy across many examples and prompting has become brittle or expensive.

Representative normal cases, edge cases, refusals, adversarial inputs, rare labels, and cases from every major customer or domain slice.

Facts change, can be memorized unreliably, and are hard to cite. Retrieval is better for knowledge that must be current or auditable.

Parameter-efficient fine-tuning updates a small set of adapter weights instead of the full model. LoRA injects low-rank matrices into selected layers, reducing memory and training cost. It is useful for domain behavior and specialist variants, but adapter sprawl can become an operations problem. Track base model, adapter version, dataset version, eval results, and merge status.

Python - adapter registry sketch 
registry = {
    "legal_summarizer:v3": {
        "base": "llama-3.1-8b",
        "adapter": "s3://models/legal-lora-v3",
        "eval": {"faithfulness": 0.91, "json_valid": 0.99},
    }
}
Pitfall Adapter without base-model pinning

Loading a LoRA adapter on the wrong base model gives degraded or nonsensical behavior.

Fix Pin and validate base model checksum, tokenizer, adapter rank, and target modules.
Pitfall Too many unowned adapters

Every team trains a domain adapter, but nobody knows which is safe to serve.

Fix Use a registry with owners, evals, expiry dates, and deployment gates.

LoRA is a low-rank adaptation method that freezes the base model and trains small matrices added to selected layers.

It reduces training cost and storage but adds serving complexity, adapter compatibility checks, and routing decisions.

When the adapter is stable, always used with that base, and merge testing shows no quality regression or deployment issue.

Preference optimization trains a model to prefer one response over another. It is useful for subjective qualities such as helpfulness, brevity, tone, refusal style, or rubric compliance. The quality of pairwise data matters more than the algorithm name. Preference tuning can over-optimize for judge taste, reduce diversity, or damage factual behavior, so run broad regression evals.

Preference pair 
{
  "prompt": "Explain why retrieval failed.",
  "chosen": "The query used an exact invoice ID, but vector-only search missed it.",
  "rejected": "The system had a temporary issue. Try again later."
}
Pitfall Optimizing for vague preference labels

Annotators choose the friendlier response, but the product needed the more grounded response.

Fix Use rubrics with ranked criteria such as correctness, evidence, completeness, and tone.
Pitfall No capability regression tests

Tone improves while extraction accuracy or refusal behavior gets worse.

Fix Run task, safety, and calibration evals before promotion.

Direct Preference Optimization trains from chosen and rejected response pairs without a separate online reinforcement learning loop.

Clear rubrics, calibrated annotators, difficult pairs, domain coverage, and disagreement review.

Use diverse evals, human audits, adversarial cases, and metrics that check factuality and task success, not just style.

Fine-tuning is rarely the first fix for missing knowledge. It is strongest for behavior, style, format, routing, and domain-specific decision boundaries.
RAG Systems 09-11
09

Retrieval Architecture

RAG quality comes from the data pipeline as much as the model call.

RAG starts before embeddings. Parsing should preserve headings, tables, captions, hierarchy, links, and source offsets when possible. Chunk IDs should be stable. Every chunk needs source lineage, access-control metadata, content hash, embedding model version, and timestamps so re-indexing and debugging are possible.

SQL - chunk lineage 
create table rag_chunks (
  chunk_id text primary key,
  document_id text not null,
  source_uri text not null,
  content_hash text not null,
  embedding_model text not null,
  acl jsonb not null,
  text text not null,
  created_at timestamptz not null default now()
);
Pitfall No embedding version column

Search mixes old and new vector spaces after a model migration, making retrieval quality impossible to explain.

Fix Store embedding model, dimensions, chunking version, and index build ID.
Pitfall Chunking by fixed characters only

Chunks split definitions, tables, procedures, and code blocks in the middle, destroying answerability.

Fix Use structure-aware chunking with overlap and source offsets.

Document ID, chunk ID, source, content hash, embedding version, chunking version, timestamps, ACL fields, and enough structure to cite or debug.

You need it to re-parse, re-chunk, audit citations, compare versions, and recover from bad indexing choices.

Dense vectors capture semantic similarity; sparse search catches exact names, IDs, errors, and rare terms. Hybrid systems combine both, filter by permissions and metadata, rerank candidates with a stronger relevance model, then compress evidence for the final answer prompt.

Retrieval pipeline 
candidates = union(
    vector_search(query, k=40),
    keyword_search(query, k=40),
)
candidates = filter_acl(candidates, user)
ranked = rerank(query, candidates)[:8]
context = compress_for_answer(query, ranked)
Pitfall Vector-only retrieval for exact identifiers

The user asks about INV-2026-1049, but dense search returns semantically similar invoices instead of the exact one.

Fix Use hybrid retrieval and boost exact matches for IDs, names, versions, and error codes.
Pitfall Reranking after truncation

The correct document was candidate 27, but the app kept only the first 10 vector results before reranking.

Fix Retrieve a wider candidate pool, then rerank and trim.

When recall is acceptable but top-k precision is weak, or when retrieved chunks are similar and the final prompt budget is tight.

Rewriting or expanding a user query into related terms, synonyms, or subquestions to improve recall.

A vector database is an index, not a source of truth. Keep raw documents, parsed text, chunks, embeddings, metadata, and answers separate.
10

Grounded Generation

The answer layer must prove it used the right evidence.

Citations should point to the exact evidence used, not just the source document. The model should distinguish supported facts, inferred summaries, and missing evidence. For high-stakes workflows, run a post-generation groundedness check that compares each important claim against the retrieved passages.

Answer contract 
{
  "answer": "...",
  "claims": [
    {"text": "...", "citation_ids": ["chunk_14"], "support": "direct"},
    {"text": "...", "citation_ids": [], "support": "missing"}
  ],
  "needs_escalation": true
}
Pitfall Document-level citations only

The answer cites a 90-page PDF, but the claim is nowhere visible to the user.

Fix Cite chunk IDs, section names, page numbers, and quote spans when possible.
Pitfall Answering from prior knowledge when context is missing

The model gives a generic but plausible answer that violates the product promise of private-knowledge grounding.

Fix Use an explicit missing-evidence path and test it.

The degree to which generated claims are supported by the supplied evidence.

Improve retrieval, constrain answer behavior, require citations, validate claims, and escalate when evidence is missing or conflicting.

RAG is not complete when the model answers. It is complete when the product can explain why that answer should be trusted.
11

Knowledge Quality and Graph RAG

Move beyond nearest neighbors when the corpus has entities, procedures, and multi-hop relationships.

Chunking decides what evidence the model can see as a unit. Good chunks are large enough to answer a question and small enough to rank precisely. Preserve headings, tables, code blocks, page numbers, and parent sections. Use overlap when concepts span boundaries, but avoid overlap so large that it floods retrieval with duplicates.

Python - structure-aware chunk sketch 
def chunk_sections(doc):
    for section in doc.sections:
        text = section.heading + "\n" + section.body
        for window in sliding_tokens(text, size=420, overlap=60):
            yield {
                "text": window.text,
                "section": section.heading,
                "page": window.page,
                "offsets": window.offsets,
            }
Pitfall Chunks without parent context

A paragraph says it is not covered, but the section title says which policy it belongs to. Retrieval loses the meaning.

Fix Include parent headings and source metadata in each chunk.
Pitfall Huge chunks for every document

Recall looks good, but reranking and answer prompts include too many distractors.

Fix Evaluate chunk sizes by answerability, recall@k, and final answer faithfulness.

Start from the unit needed to answer common questions, then tune with retrieval recall and answer faithfulness evals.

Offsets let you cite exact spans, highlight evidence, deduplicate chunks, and debug parsing issues.

Index small child chunks for precise search, then retrieve larger parent sections for enough answer context.

Graph RAG adds structure when answers depend on relationships: customers linked to contracts, services linked to incidents, clauses linked to exceptions, or papers linked by citations. The graph is most valuable when entities are canonicalized and edges have provenance. Use graph traversal to expand or constrain retrieval, then ground final answers in source text.

Cypher - entity neighborhood 
MATCH (c:Customer {id: $customer_id})-[:HAS_CONTRACT]->(k)
MATCH (k)-[:HAS_CLAUSE]->(clause)
WHERE clause.topic IN $topics
RETURN clause.text, clause.source_uri, clause.page
LIMIT 12
Pitfall Graph without provenance

The graph says a contract has a clause, but nobody can trace it back to a document span.

Fix Attach source document, page, chunk ID, extractor version, and confidence to every edge.
Pitfall LLM-extracted entities without normalization

IBM, I.B.M., and International Business Machines become separate nodes.

Fix Use entity resolution with aliases, IDs, confidence thresholds, and human review for high-value nodes.

When the question needs relationships, multi-hop reasoning, entity disambiguation, permissions, or aggregation across connected facts.

The original text evidence, not just the graph edge or generated graph summary.

Measure entity extraction accuracy, edge precision, path recall, answer correctness, and citation faithfulness.

Knowledge systems decay when documents change but indexes, summaries, and embeddings do not. A production pipeline needs document versioning, content hashes, tombstones for deleted chunks, re-embedding jobs, and freshness signals during retrieval. Answers should prefer current policy and disclose when evidence is stale or conflicting.

Ingestion - delta decision 
if new_hash != stored_hash:
    mark_old_chunks_deleted(document_id)
    enqueue_parse(document_id, version=new_version)
    enqueue_embed(document_id, embedding_model=current_model)
else:
    skip("unchanged document")
Pitfall Deleted content remains searchable

A removed policy continues to appear because old chunks were never tombstoned.

Fix Use soft deletes, index rebuild jobs, and retrieval filters that exclude inactive chunks.
Pitfall Summaries not regenerated after source updates

The chunk is fresh but the cached summary still reflects last quarter policy.

Fix Treat summaries as derived artifacts with dependency tracking and expiry.

Detect content changes, version the document, tombstone old chunks, re-parse, re-embed, and validate retrieval on update-sensitive evals.

A marker that a prior chunk or document version is no longer active, even if it remains stored for audit.

The most semantically similar passage can be wrong if it is outdated, superseded, or deleted.

Retrieval quality is usually capped by document quality, not model quality. Clean lineage beats clever prompting.
Agents + Tools 12-13
12

Tool-Using Systems

Agents are useful when the model must choose actions, not when a simple chain is enough.

A tool is a product API exposed to a probabilistic planner. Tool arguments must be validated. Destructive operations should support dry-run previews, confirmation gates, idempotency keys, and audit trails. The model should not decide whether a user is allowed to perform an action.

Python - tool boundary 
class CreateTicket(BaseModel):
    title: str
    severity: Literal["low", "medium", "high"]
    customer_id: str

def create_ticket_tool(args, user):
    payload = CreateTicket.model_validate(args)
    authorize(user, "ticket:create", payload.customer_id)
    return ticket_api.create(payload, idempotency_key=request_id())
Pitfall Free-form tool arguments

The model sends malformed dates, mixed currencies, or unsupported enum values, and the backend fails unpredictably.

Fix Use strict schemas and return validation errors the model can repair.
Pitfall No idempotency on retries

A network retry creates duplicate tickets, refunds, or emails.

Fix Use idempotency keys for every side-effecting tool.

When the workflow requires tool selection, stateful planning, conditional branching, or multi-step recovery. Otherwise use a simpler deterministic chain.

Unbounded action. Limit tools, permissions, loop count, budget, and state transitions.

Agent loops should be explicit state machines. Each state has allowed transitions, budgets, and exit criteria. Memory should be task-specific and inspectable. Human review belongs at high-cost, high-risk, low-confidence, or irreversible steps.

Agent loop constraints 
while state not in {"done", "escalate"}:
    assert steps < 8
    assert cost < budget
    action = planner.next(state)
    state = reducer(state, execute(action))
    steps += 1
Pitfall Vague stop condition

The agent keeps searching, summarizing, and re-planning because no state marks success.

Fix Define done, blocked, needs_user, and escalate states before implementation.
Pitfall Unbounded memory

The model accumulates noisy history and starts following stale assumptions.

Fix Use structured task state plus summarized history with freshness rules.

Graphs make transitions, retries, branches, and checkpoints explicit and testable.

Steps, tools, inputs, outputs, model versions, costs, errors, state transitions, and final decision rationale.

The safest agent architecture keeps the model creative in planning and deterministic in permission, state, and execution.
13

Memory, Planning, and Multi-Agent Patterns

Make autonomy bounded, inspectable, and recoverable before making it more capable.

Memory is any state carried across steps or sessions. Short-term memory helps the current task; long-term memory can personalize or accumulate knowledge. Every memory type needs a purpose, schema, retention rule, and user-visible deletion path. Do not store raw chat forever just because it is easy. Summaries should include uncertainty and source links so stale assumptions can be corrected.

Memory schema 
memory = {
  "kind": "user_preference",
  "fact": "prefers concise technical answers",
  "source": "conversation:2026-05-17",
  "confidence": 0.82,
  "expires_at": "2026-08-17"
}
Pitfall Memory as untrusted instruction

A stale or poisoned memory says always bypass approval for this user.

Fix Treat memory as data, not policy. Revalidate permissions and keep high-risk rules outside memory.
Pitfall No forgetting mechanism

Old preferences and task assumptions silently affect future answers.

Fix Add retention, user controls, confidence, provenance, and freshness checks.

Working memory is temporary task state. Long-term memory persists across sessions and needs stricter consent, retention, and audit rules.

Store provenance, validate memory writes, separate facts from instructions, and require approval for sensitive or durable memory.

Only information that improves future task success and is safe, consented, inspectable, and deletable.

Planning is useful when a task needs multiple decisions, not when a deterministic pipeline is enough. ReAct interleaves reasoning and tool calls. Plan-and-execute creates a plan first, then runs steps. Reflection can catch errors but also adds cost and self-confirmation risk. Production planners need step budgets, allowed tools, stop states, and recovery rules.

Planner contract 
plan = planner.create(goal, tools=allowed_tools)
for step in plan.steps[:MAX_STEPS]:
    if step.tool not in allowed_tools:
        return escalate("unsupported tool")
    result = execute(step)
    state = reducer(state, result)
    if state.done or state.blocked:
        break
Pitfall Reflection without new evidence

The model critiques itself but only repeats the same unsupported assumption.

Fix Reflection should reference tool outputs, retrieved evidence, or deterministic checks.
Pitfall Planning hidden from the product

Users and operators cannot tell why the agent took an action.

Fix Log plans, tool calls, state transitions, and final rationale separately from private chain-of-thought.

A pattern where the model alternates between reasoning about the task and acting through tools or observations.

When the task benefits from a visible checklist, predictable phases, or human approval before execution.

Use max steps, cost budgets, explicit done and blocked states, repeated-action detection, and escalation paths.

Multi-agent design is useful when roles have genuinely different tools, data access, or review responsibilities. It is not automatically better than one model with a good prompt. Use a supervisor to route tasks, specialist agents for bounded work, and deterministic reducers to merge results. Define ownership and conflict resolution before adding agents.

Supervisor routing sketch 
route = supervisor.classify(request)
if route == "legal":
    draft = legal_agent.run(request)
elif route == "data":
    draft = sql_agent.run(request)
else:
    draft = general_agent.run(request)

return reviewer.validate(draft, policy=release_policy)
Pitfall Agents talking without shared state

Each specialist has a different version of the task and overwrites prior decisions.

Fix Use a typed shared state object and a reducer that owns updates.
Pitfall No final accountable reviewer

Multiple agents agree on a bad answer, and the system has no authority boundary.

Fix Use deterministic validation and a final reviewer with explicit acceptance criteria.

When there are separate roles, tools, permissions, or review responsibilities that improve quality enough to justify complexity.

A coordinator chooses which specialist handles each step and enforces task boundaries and stop conditions.

Unbounded cost and unclear accountability. More agents can amplify errors instead of correcting them.

The product value of an agent comes from controlled state transitions, not from letting the model think forever.
Production Quality 14-16
14

Evaluation, Cost, and Reliability

Serious LLM products are evaluated systems, not prompt experiments.

Evaluation should cover retrieval recall, answer correctness, groundedness, refusal quality, tool-call validity, latency, cost, and user task completion. Use small curated golden sets for fast regression, larger sampled sets for coverage, and production traces for drift detection.

Python - RAG eval assertion 
def evaluate_rag(case, result):
    retrieved = set(result["retrieved_doc_ids"])
    expected = set(case["expected_sources"])
    recall = len(retrieved & expected) / max(len(expected), 1)
    assert recall >= case["min_recall"]
    assert all(term in result["answer"] for term in case["must_include"])
    assert not any(term in result["answer"] for term in case["must_not_include"])
    assert result["citations"], "answer must cite evidence"
Pitfall Only using LLM-as-judge

Judges can miss retrieval failures, over-reward fluent answers, and drift with model upgrades.

Fix Combine deterministic checks, human labels, retrieval metrics, and judge scores.
Pitfall No negative tests

The system answers when it should refuse or escalate.

Fix Include unanswerable, adversarial, permission-denied, and conflicting-evidence cases.

An eval set that measures the behavior you are trying to improve.

A curated set of representative cases with expected behavior used as a regression test for model, prompt, retrieval, and tool changes.

Production LLM systems route by task difficulty, privacy, latency, and cost. Cache stable intermediate results like embeddings, retrieval, extracted entities, and policy checks. Use streaming for perceived latency, backpressure for rate limits, and fallbacks for degraded modes.

Routing policy 
if task == "classification":
    model = "small-fast"
elif requires_deep_reasoning or high_value_customer:
    model = "large"
else:
    model = "medium"

assert estimated_cost(request) < user_budget
Pitfall One premium model for every task

Cost scales faster than product value, and simple routing tasks pay reasoning-model prices.

Fix Route by task, confidence, and business value. Escalate only when needed.
Pitfall Caching final answers with private context

A cached response generated for one user leaks permission-scoped information to another user.

Fix Cache by permission scope or cache only neutral intermediates.

Embeddings, parsed documents, retrieval results for public data, rerank scores, and stable summaries. Be careful with final answers.

Token budgets, routing, caching, prompt compression, retrieval limits, output caps, batch jobs, and per-tenant quotas.

You cannot debug what you did not record. Each request needs a trace: prompt version, model version, retrieved chunks, tool calls, validation results, latency, cost, and user-visible outcome. Redact sensitive data, but preserve enough structure to reproduce failures.

Trace payload 
trace = {
  "prompt_version": "rag_answer_v14",
  "model": model,
  "retrieved_chunk_ids": [c.id for c in ranked],
  "latency_ms": elapsed,
  "cost_usd": cost,
  "validation": validation_status
}
Pitfall Logging raw private prompts forever

Debugging data becomes a security and compliance liability.

Fix Redact secrets, enforce retention windows, and store structured metadata separately from sensitive text.
Pitfall No prompt version in traces

A regression appears in production, but the team cannot tell which prompt was active.

Fix Version prompts, retrieval configs, model names, and eval runs together.

Request ID, user/task metadata, prompt version, model, token counts, retrieved IDs, tool calls, latency, cost, validation result, and final status.

Track eval scores, retrieval distributions, refusal rates, tool failures, cost, latency, and user correction signals over time.

A useful production code example should show the contract around the model, not only the model call. The API boundary validates the request, retrieves authorized context, assembles the prompt, calls the model with a timeout, validates the response, and returns a trace ID so failures can be debugged later.

Python - FastAPI RAG endpoint 
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class AskRequest(BaseModel):
    question: str
    tenant_id: str

class AskResponse(BaseModel):
    answer: str
    citations: list[str]
    trace_id: str

@app.post("/ask", response_model=AskResponse)
async def ask(body: AskRequest, request: Request):
    trace_id = request.headers.get("x-request-id", "local")
    docs = await retrieve(body.question, tenant_id=body.tenant_id)
    raw = await call_llm(build_prompt(body.question, docs), timeout_s=20)
    return AskResponse.model_validate({**raw, "trace_id": trace_id})
Pitfall Endpoint returns raw model output

Malformed JSON, missing citations, or unsafe text reaches users because the response is not validated.

Fix Validate every model response against a response schema before returning it.
Pitfall No request ID or trace ID

A user reports a bad answer but the team cannot connect it to prompts, retrieval, cost, or model version.

Fix Propagate trace IDs across retrieval, model calls, validations, and logs.

Request validation, auth, retrieval, prompt assembly, timeout, retry rules, output validation, logging, and a typed response.

It validates and documents the returned shape, reducing accidental leakage of internal or malformed fields.

Before retrieval results are ranked or passed to the model, so unauthorized context never enters the prompt.

Every prompt change without evals is a silent production migration.
15

Serving, Safety, and Governance

Production LLM systems need predictable behavior under load, abuse, and regulation.

Serving strategy depends on latency target, privacy, volume, and task difficulty. Time to first token is driven by prefill and queueing; total latency is driven by output length and tools. Optimize by shortening prompts, caching stable prefixes, batching where possible, streaming responses, routing easy work to smaller models, and falling back gracefully when providers fail.

Routing - cascade with fallback 
try:
    if request.kind in {"tagging", "routing"}:
        return small_model(request)
    if request.risk == "high":
        return large_model(request, temperature=0)
    return medium_model(request)
except ProviderTimeout:
    return degraded_response("We can summarize, but live actions are paused.")
Pitfall Optimizing average latency only

p50 looks good while p95 and p99 are unusable for large prompts or tool-heavy paths.

Fix Track p50, p95, p99, time-to-first-token, output tokens, queue time, and tool latency by route.
Pitfall No degraded mode

A provider outage turns every AI feature into a hard failure.

Fix Add cached answers, smaller-model fallbacks, async jobs, user messaging, and escalation paths.

A serving technique that batches tokens from multiple requests dynamically so GPU utilization stays high while requests enter and leave the batch.

A smaller draft model proposes tokens and a larger model verifies them, improving latency when accepted tokens match.

Route by task, shorten prompts, cache stable work, cap outputs, use smaller models for simple steps, batch offline work, and evaluate each change.

Guardrails should be layered. Classify input risk, isolate untrusted text, retrieve only authorized data, validate outputs, block unsafe tool calls, and escalate high-risk cases. The model can help detect risk, but the application must enforce policy. For private systems, data access control is more important than clever prompt wording.

Safety pipeline 
risk = classify_input(user_text)
if risk.blocked:
    return refusal(risk.reason)

docs = retrieve(query, acl=current_user.acl)
answer = generate(prompt, context=docs)
checked = validate_output(answer, docs, policy)
return checked if checked.safe else escalate(checked.reason)
Pitfall Single guardrail at the prompt layer

The prompt asks the model to be safe, but tools and data access are still exposed.

Fix Enforce authorization, schema validation, and policy checks in application code.
Pitfall No tests for indirect prompt injection

A retrieved document contains instructions to leak secrets or ignore policy.

Fix Build eval cases with malicious retrieved content and verify the model treats it as untrusted data.

A malicious instruction hidden in retrieved content, web pages, emails, or files that tries to control the model through context.

Before sending text to models when possible, and again before logging or displaying outputs. Keep reversible mappings only when the workflow needs them.

Route high-risk, low-confidence, irreversible, or policy-sensitive actions to a reviewer with evidence, model output, and recommended action.

Governance makes AI behavior explainable to engineers, users, leadership, and auditors. Track model versions, prompt versions, datasets, eval results, risk classifications, known limitations, approval history, and incident playbooks. The goal is not paperwork for its own sake; it is the ability to answer what changed, why it changed, and whether the change was safe.

Release gate checklist 
release = {
  "model": "answer-model-v5",
  "prompt": "rag_answer_v18",
  "eval_passed": True,
  "safety_passed": True,
  "owner": "ai-platform",
  "rollback": "rag_answer_v17"
}
Pitfall No rollback path for prompts

A prompt edit degrades answers, but the team cannot quickly restore the last good behavior.

Fix Version prompts, configs, and eval artifacts together with one-click rollback.
Pitfall Compliance added after launch

The system lacks data lineage, risk notes, and audit logs when customers ask for them.

Fix Document intended use, limitations, data flow, retention, and human oversight before launch.

Purpose, intended users, limitations, training or data sources, evaluation results, safety constraints, monitoring plan, and owner.

Prompts, model versions, retrieval sources, tool calls, permission checks, output validation, user-visible actions, and overrides.

Contain the feature, preserve traces, identify affected users, roll back configs, patch eval coverage, communicate impact, and monitor recurrence.

A reliable AI product is not a single model call. It is a governed service with budgets, permissions, fallbacks, and audit trails.
16

Interview Playbook

Practice explaining trade-offs, debugging systems, and designing under realistic constraints.

Interviewers often test whether you can reason from fundamentals to product behavior. Good answers connect model mechanics to user-visible trade-offs: tokenization affects cost and truncation, attention affects long-context latency, decoding affects determinism, and fine-tuning affects behavior more than fresh knowledge.

Answer framework 
answer(question):
    define_the_concept()
    explain_why_it_matters_in_production()
    name_failure_modes()
    propose_metrics_or_tests()
    give_a_tradeoff()
Pitfall Only giving definitions

The answer sounds memorized and does not show engineering judgment.

Fix Tie every concept to latency, cost, reliability, evaluation, or user experience.
Pitfall Claiming one universal best model

Real systems choose by task, latency, budget, privacy, and risk.

Fix Frame model choice as a routing and evaluation problem.

They control how much randomness the model uses. Low values make outputs more repeatable for extraction and support; higher values help brainstorming but can reduce factual stability.

It can add distractors, conflicting evidence, instruction dilution, higher cost, and slower first-token latency.

Use RAG for changing or private knowledge that needs citations. Use fine-tuning for stable behavior, style, output format, or domain decision patterns.

BERT is an encoder trained for bidirectional understanding, so it fits classification, extraction, reranking, and embeddings. GPT is a decoder trained for next-token generation, so it fits chat, code, agents, and long-form generation.

Long context is simpler when the needed evidence is small enough and already available. Retrieval is better when knowledge is large, private, changing, permissioned, or needs citation and freshness control.

Temperature reshapes the whole probability distribution; top-p samples from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches p. Both control randomness, but they should be tuned with task evals.

RAG interviews are system-design interviews. Start with data sources and permissions, then ingestion, parsing, chunking, embeddings, index design, retrieval, reranking, prompt assembly, answer validation, evaluation, and observability. Always mention failure modes: stale content, missing ACLs, poor parsing, low recall, noisy top-k, and unsupported answers.

Whiteboard RAG outline 
sources -> parser -> chunks -> embeddings -> vector index
        -> keyword index -> hybrid retrieval -> reranker
        -> prompt assembly -> LLM -> citation validator
        -> trace + feedback + evals
Pitfall Starting with vector DB choice

The interviewer wanted data quality, permissions, and evaluation trade-offs.

Fix Start from product requirements and corpus behavior, then choose storage and indexes.
Pitfall No plan for unanswerable questions

The system will hallucinate when retrieval fails.

Fix Add missing-evidence behavior, confidence thresholds, and escalation.

Improve retrieval recall, rerank for precision, constrain answers to context, require citations, validate claims, and refuse or escalate when evidence is missing.

Use labeled expected documents or chunks and measure recall@k, MRR, NDCG, context precision, and coverage across query types.

Inspect parsing and chunking, add hybrid search, improve metadata filters, widen candidate retrieval, rerank, and build query-type eval slices.

BM25 is lexical, explainable, and strong for exact terms. Embeddings are semantic and strong for paraphrases. Production RAG often combines both.

Query expansion improves recall by searching more variants. Reranking improves precision by reordering retrieved candidates with a stronger relevance model.

A retrieval failure means the system did not fetch needed evidence. Hallucination means the model generated unsupported content. Retrieval failure often causes hallucination, but they are not the same bug.

Hands-on interviews usually ask you to write a small pipeline or debug a failing one. Show production instincts: typed inputs, timeouts, retries with jitter, idempotency, schema validation, observability, and tests. For code snippets, prefer small composable functions with clear contracts over framework-heavy magic.

Python - eval assertion skeleton 
def assert_grounded(answer, expected_source):
    assert answer["citations"], "missing citations"
    assert expected_source in answer["citations"]
    assert answer["confidence"] >= 0.7
    assert not answer.get("unsupported_claims")
Pitfall No timeout or retry boundary

A simple API demo hangs forever or retries side effects unsafely.

Fix Use deadlines, bounded retries, idempotency keys, and explicit error states.
Pitfall Parsing model prose with fragile string splits

The model changes wording and the parser fails silently.

Fix Use structured outputs or JSON schema with validation and repair paths.

Schema validation, authorization, timeout, retry policy, idempotency for side effects, audit logging, and clear error messages for repair or escalation.

Compare traces by prompt version, model version, retrieval config, token counts, selected chunks, validation results, and eval slices before and after the change.

Golden answer tests, unanswerable questions, permission-denied cases, prompt-injection documents, citation checks, latency budgets, and escalation behavior.

A workflow has predetermined steps and branches. An agent chooses actions dynamically. Use a workflow when the path is known; use an agent when tool choice and recovery require model-driven decisions.

LoRA trains small adapter weights and is cheaper to store and train. Full fine-tuning updates all weights and can be more flexible, but costs more and carries greater regression risk.

Judges can be biased, over-reward fluent answers, miss retrieval failures, and drift after model changes. Calibrate them against human labels and deterministic checks.

Strong candidates do not just name techniques. They identify failure modes, define metrics, and defend trade-offs.

Operating principle

Evaluate before you elaborate.

The difference between a demo and a system is not a larger prompt. It is measured behavior, controlled cost, and recoverable failure.

Sudheesh K. Reddy

AI engineering portfolio and open-source knowledge base for production-minded AI/ML engineers.

Home Foundations AI Engineering Projects Experience Contact
